metadata.design_notes: Architectural questions that test reasoning about design trade-offs, cross-module interactions, and system-level properties. Answers exist in DERIVED beliefs but require reading multiple files and synthesizing judgment from code. Designed to test H5: beliefs help more on architectural questions than factual ones.

questions.text: What is the primary architectural weakness of the capability registry?

choices.a: It has no persistence layer — capabilities are lost on restart
choices.b: It depends on global ID uniqueness but uses last-write-wins semantics with no collision detection
choices.c: It cannot handle more than 100 registered capabilities
choices.d: It requires manual ordering of capability registration

questions.text: How would you characterize the planner's design trade-off?

choices.a: Fast but inaccurate — it sacrifices routing quality for speed
choices.b: Safe but architecturally wasteful — it explores within bounds but over-selects capabilities
choices.c: Accurate but slow — it does exhaustive search at the cost of latency
choices.d: Simple but inflexible — it uses hard-coded rules instead of LLM-based routing

questions.text: What asymmetric safety strategy does the citation subsystem use?

choices.a: Strict validation at construction, lenient at output
choices.b: Permissive at construction (validation deferred to format time), defensive at extraction (strategies never raise, deduplication)
choices.c: No validation at any stage — relies entirely on Guardian
choices.d: Identical validation at both construction and extraction

questions.text: How does the system enforce data correctness across the pipeline?

choices.a: Through runtime assertions and contract tests
choices.b: Through database transactions and rollback
choices.c: Through pervasive immutability — frozen citations, immutable department trees, write-once pipeline state
choices.d: Through input validation at the API boundary only

questions.text: What is the error handling philosophy gradient across the system?

choices.a: All errors are logged and swallowed — the system never crashes
choices.b: All errors are raised — fail-fast everywhere
choices.c: Fail-hard for infrastructure, fail-soft for individual records, fail-open for service unavailability, fail-closed for ambiguous security
choices.d: Errors are retried three times then raised to the caller

questions.text: How do trust enforcement mechanisms differ between the planner and Guardian?

choices.a: They are redundant — both do the same trust checks at different times
choices.b: Planner enforces structural trust hierarchy at routing time (departments > dataverse > external tools); Guardian enforces empirical trust at output time via asymmetric error policy
choices.c: Planner trusts all sources equally; Guardian is the only trust mechanism
choices.d: Guardian handles routing trust; planner handles output validation

questions.text: What is the remaining efficiency improvement frontier for the system?

choices.a: Database query optimization — slow queries dominate latency
choices.b: Model selection — using smaller models would reduce cost
choices.c: Trust-cost amplification — planner waste invisibly amplifies the cost of trust enforcement (Guardian, citations)
choices.d: Network latency — API calls to external services dominate

questions.text: Why can't the evaluation framework detect computational waste from over-exploration?

choices.a: Evaluation only runs in test mode which skips the planner
choices.b: Eval scoring rewards correct department selection without penalizing unnecessary selections, and irrevocable forward-chaining means waste occurs silently after routing
choices.c: The evaluation framework doesn't have access to timing data
choices.d: Waste is not a concern because the system is already optimized

questions.text: What two independent correctness layers protect citations from extraction through output?

choices.a: Input validation and output sanitization
choices.b: Citation pipeline defense-in-depth (defensive extraction, immutable types, synthesis preservation) AND Guardian validation (comprehensive checks, asymmetric error policy)
choices.c: Schema validation and runtime type checking
choices.d: Unit tests and integration tests

questions.text: How does the DI/config architectural backbone enable both quality infrastructure and trust enforcement?

choices.a: By using environment variables for all configuration
choices.b: By providing factory seams for test doubles (hermetic environments) and multi-mechanism trust enforcement (immutability, Guardian, planner hierarchy)
choices.c: By using a centralized configuration database
choices.d: By enforcing strict typing across all interfaces

questions.text: What is the MCP subsystem's architectural limitation despite being well-designed?

choices.a: It only supports HTTP transport, not WebSocket
choices.b: It is architecturally sound (layered transport, fault-tolerant client) but has an inherent performance ceiling from N+1 sequential enrichment during discovery
choices.c: It cannot handle more than 10 concurrent connections
choices.d: It requires a dedicated MCP server per department

questions.text: How does Guardian's error policy differ between data errors and API errors?

choices.a: Both use the same retry-then-fail policy
choices.b: Fail-closed for missing data and ambiguous parsing (conservative), fail-open for API/service errors (availability)
choices.c: Fail-open for everything — availability is always prioritized
choices.d: Data errors are retried; API errors are logged and ignored

questions.text: What pattern does the system use for dependency injection across its major components?

choices.a: A DI container (like Java Spring) manages all dependencies
choices.b: Factory-based injection: centralized Model.from_config(), constructor-injected execute_fn, factory-created LangGraph nodes, callback-injected planner delegation
choices.c: Service locator pattern with a global registry
choices.d: All dependencies are hard-coded — no injection is used

questions.text: What makes the evaluation framework's quality assessment incomplete?

choices.a: It only tests the planner, not the executor or synthesizer
choices.b: Planner routing quality is measurable across four dimensions, but the broader framework has latent fragility from LangFlow removal and incomplete iteration support
choices.c: It requires manual scoring — no automated evaluation exists
choices.d: It only works with Gemini models, not Claude

questions.text: How does the system ensure concurrent access safety across process boundaries and in-memory caches?

choices.a: It uses database-level locks for all shared state
choices.b: Cross-process auth uses fcntl file locking, Dataverse token refresh uses double-checked asyncio.Lock, eval scoring uses thread-safe caches with Event sentinels
choices.c: It avoids concurrency entirely — all operations are sequential
choices.d: It uses Redis distributed locks for coordination

questions.text: What is the configuration cascade pattern used across the planner and synthesizer?

choices.a: Environment variables override config files override defaults
choices.b: State-over-config-over-default: runtime state overrides configuration, which overrides defaults — applied identically in both subsystems
choices.c: Configuration is loaded once at startup and never changes
choices.d: Each subsystem has its own independent configuration with no shared pattern

questions.text: How does the Drive subsystem achieve security across read, write, and storage operations?

choices.a: All operations go through a single authenticated proxy
choices.b: Read traversal is bounded (depth limits, cycle detection, department isolation); writes are triple-guarded (scope gates, upsert semantics, re-auth); storage uses defensive error absorption with exponential backoff
choices.c: OAuth tokens are validated on every API call
choices.d: Read-only mode is enforced — writes are not supported

questions.text: How does department tree structure emerge at runtime?

choices.a: From a hierarchy database table loaded at startup
choices.b: From explicit parent_id fields in each department config
choices.c: From dot-delimited ID strings — hierarchy is computed from IDs with conditional self-registration, not stored
choices.d: From a hierarchy.yaml file that maps parent-child relationships

questions.text: What makes the full query lifecycle structurally sound from planning through citation output?

choices.a: Comprehensive unit test coverage validates every path
choices.b: Planning produces validated, budget-constrained capability selections that execute correctly under concurrency, and citations maintain semantic integrity from extraction through final formatting
choices.c: A single transaction wraps the entire query lifecycle
choices.d: Runtime assertions check invariants at every pipeline stage

questions.text: Why does the pipeline startup avoid heavy initialization?

choices.a: To reduce memory usage for serverless deployment
choices.b: Nodes use lazy imports, Department.execute delegates to MCP (not implemented locally), and the pipeline is code-defined not JSON-parsed
choices.c: The pipeline is precompiled and cached between requests
choices.d: All initialization is deferred to a background thread
